content.body:
## What is sound?

* Wave (vibration in air) — continuous, **1D** (varies over time only)
* Compare: images are 2D (x and y)

**Frequency (Hz):** physical measurement → **Pitch:** human perception

👉 High frequency = high pitch

Real sound = **fundamental + harmonics** — harmonics give sound its character

---

## Digitization

$$\text{Sound} \rightarrow \text{Microphone} \rightarrow \text{ADC} \rightarrow \text{Digital}$$

### Two steps

**1. Sampling** — take values at regular time intervals
* Parameter: **sampling rate (fs)** in Hz

**2. Quantization** — round values to discrete levels
* Parameter: **bits (N)**

---

## Nyquist Theorem (CRITICAL)

**The problem — Aliasing:**
* Sampling too slow → wrong frequency appears → signal misrepresented

$$f_s \ge 2 f_{max}$$

Sampling rate must be **at least twice** the highest frequency.

$$f_{Nyquist} = \frac{f_s}{2}$$

Maximum frequency you can correctly capture.

$$f_{alias} = f_s - f_{true}$$

Frequency that appears when aliasing occurs.

---

## Example 1: Minimum sampling rate

Signal max frequency = 5 kHz

$$f_s \ge 2 \times 5000 = 10{,}000 \text{ Hz}$$

Need at least **10 kHz** sampling rate.

---

## Example 2: Detecting aliasing

* Signal = 7 kHz, Sampling = 10 kHz

$$f_{Nyquist} = 10{,}000 \div 2 = 5{,}000 \text{ Hz}$$

7 kHz > 5 kHz → **Aliasing occurs!**

$$f_{alias} = 10{,}000 - 7{,}000 = 3{,}000 \text{ Hz}$$

The 7 kHz signal appears as **3 kHz**

flashcards.q: What is sound, physically? How is it different from an image signal?
flashcards.a: Sound is a **pressure wave** — air molecules compressed and expanded by a vibrating source. It is a **1-dimensional signal** (amplitude varies over time only). Images are 2D (amplitude varies over x and y position).

flashcards.q: What is the difference between frequency and pitch?
flashcards.a: **Frequency** is objective — it measures cycles per second (Hz). **Pitch** is subjective — it is how high or low a sound *feels* to a listener. A sound at 440 Hz always has that frequency, but pitch perception can vary by context.

flashcards.q: What are harmonics, and why do they matter?
flashcards.a: Harmonics are **integer multiples of a fundamental frequency**. Example: 440 Hz → 880, 1320, 1760 Hz. Real instruments produce a mixture of harmonics — the specific mix gives each instrument its **timbre** (sound color). This is why different instruments sound different on the same note.

flashcards.q: What is the difference between sampling and quantization?
flashcards.a: **Sampling** works in the **time domain** — it decides *how often* to measure. **Quantization** works in the **amplitude domain** — it decides *how precisely* to measure each value by rounding to the nearest of $2^N$ levels.

flashcards.q: What does the Nyquist Theorem say, exactly?
flashcards.a: The **sampling rate must be at least twice the maximum frequency** in the signal: $f_{sampling} \geq 2 \times f_{max}$. Below this rate, aliasing occurs — you measure a wrong (lower) frequency instead of the real one.

flashcards.q: What is aliasing? What causes it?
flashcards.a: Aliasing is when a signal is sampled too slowly and the samples appear to represent a **different, lower frequency** — an 'alias' of the real signal. Cause: $f_{sampling} < 2 \times f_{true}$. The alias frequency is $f_{alias} = f_{sampling} - f_{true}$.

flashcards.q: What is the Nyquist frequency, and how is it different from the Nyquist rate?
flashcards.a:
- **Nyquist rate** = $2 \times f_{max}$ — the *minimum sampling rate* you need to avoid aliasing
- **Nyquist frequency** = $f_{sampling}/2$ — the *highest frequency* you can correctly capture at a given sampling rate

flashcards.q: Why does CD audio use 44,100 Hz if human hearing only goes to 20 kHz?
flashcards.a: Nyquist rate = 2 × 20,000 = 40,000 Hz. CD uses 44,100 Hz — **above the minimum** — for two reasons: (1) a safety margin in case some content approaches 20 kHz, and (2) to give the anti-aliasing filter room to roll off smoothly without cutting audible frequencies.

problems.q: What is the minimum sampling rate needed to correctly capture a signal with maximum frequency 5 kHz?
problems.a:
$f_s \ge 2 \times f_{max} = 2 \times 5{,}000 = \mathbf{10{,}000 \text{ Hz}}$

Need at least 10 kHz.

problems.q: A 7 kHz signal is sampled at 10 kHz. Does aliasing occur? If so, what alias frequency appears?
problems.a:
$f_{Nyquist} = 10{,}000 \div 2 = 5{,}000$ Hz

7 kHz > 5 kHz → **Yes, aliasing occurs!**

$f_{alias} = f_s - f_{true} = 10{,}000 - 7{,}000 = \mathbf{3{,}000 \text{ Hz}}$

The 7 kHz signal appears as 3 kHz.
problems.hint: Check: is f > fNyquist? Then alias = fs − f.

problems.q: Human hearing range is 20 Hz to 20 kHz. What minimum sampling rate is needed? What does CD audio use and why?
problems.a:
$f_s \ge 2 \times 20{,}000 = \mathbf{40{,}000 \text{ Hz}}$

CD audio uses **44,100 Hz** — slightly above the 40 kHz minimum, with extra margin to allow for a practical anti-aliasing filter roll-off.
problems.hint: fmax = 20 kHz. Nyquist says double it. CD adds a safety margin.

problems.q: A signal at 12 kHz is sampled at 10 kHz. What alias frequency appears?
problems.a:
$f_{Nyquist} = 10{,}000 \div 2 = 5{,}000$ Hz

12 kHz > 5 kHz → aliasing occurs

$f_{alias} = 10{,}000 - 12{,}000 = -2{,}000$ Hz

Take absolute value: **2 kHz** appears.
problems.hint: If alias = fs − f is negative, take the absolute value.

problems.q: A signal contains three frequencies: 2 kHz, 5 kHz, and 9 kHz. What is the minimum sampling rate needed?
problems.a:
Nyquist applies to the **highest frequency** component.

$f_{max} = 9 \text{ kHz}$

$f_s \ge 2 \times 9{,}000 = \mathbf{18{,}000 \text{ Hz}}$

Need at least 18 kHz — must cover all frequencies present.
problems.hint: Nyquist uses fmax — the highest frequency in the signal.

problems.q: A 20 kHz signal is sampled at 18 kHz. Does aliasing occur? If so, what alias frequency appears?
problems.a:
$f_{Nyquist} = 18{,}000 \div 2 = 9{,}000$ Hz

20 kHz > 9 kHz → **Yes, aliasing occurs!**

$f_{alias} = 18{,}000 - 20{,}000 = -2{,}000$ Hz → absolute value: **2 kHz**

The 20 kHz signal appears as 2 kHz.
problems.hint: fNyquist = fs/2. If signal > fNyquist, alias = |fs − f|.
